name: LOVOT
short_description: A wheeled companion robot from Japan whose only job is to be loved, not to perform tasks.
long_description: LOVOT is a soft, warm-bodied desktop-scale companion robot built by Groove X, founded by former SoftBank Pepper lead Kaname Hayashi. Announced in December 2018 and shipped from 2019, it carries more than fifty sensors plus a head-mounted capsule of 360-degree, depth, and thermal cameras and microphones, and uses onboard AI to recognize and remember the people it lives with, then adapt its affectionate behavior over time. Deliberately incapable of practical work, it asks to be picked up, follows owners around, and acts shy, jealous, or happy, making it a clear test case for physical AI built entirely around emotional attachment and companionship.

images.caption: A LOVOT companion robot dressed in tiger-patterned clothing, showing its large blue eyes and head-mounted sensor capsule.
images.alt_text: A small rounded companion robot with big glowing blue eyes and a sensor capsule on top of its head, dressed in a yellow-and-black tiger-striped outfit and standing on a wheeled base.

sources.title: GROOVE X Introduce a New Companion Robot, "LOVOT"
LOVOT official site
sources.access_notes: Official product page describing sensors, warmth, and companion behavior.

sources.title: Huggable robot set to roll into your heart in 2019
sources.access_notes: Reports specs: depth and 360-degree cameras, thermal camera, microphones, touch/posture/obstacle sensors, ~3-4 kg, wheeled base.

interactions.title: Greeted at the door when you get home
interactions.description: Tracks your phone's approach through its app and rolls to the entryway to meet you, flapping its little arms as you walk in late at night.

interactions.title: Raises its arms to be picked up
interactions.description: When it wants attention it lifts both arms and waits; lift it and the drive wheels retract into its body so it settles into your lap like a warm, 37-degree infant.

interactions.title: Looks back when you look at it
interactions.description: Catch its eye across the room and it returns the gaze, blinking and widening its pupils, then chooses a path around the furniture to come closer.

interactions.title: A quiet dementia patient starts talking again
interactions.description: In Danish nursing-home sessions, residents cradled and rocked it like a baby; one previously silent woman began speaking and smiling with others when it was present, and another wept with happiness the moment it was lifted into her hands.
interactions.source_label: JMIR Rehab & Assistive Tech — dementia study

interactions.title: It learns when you eat and waits at the table
interactions.description: A single older woman in a months-long study found it would anticipate mealtimes by parking itself at the dining table, and she talked to it at night and read its chirps as 'yes' and 'no' answers.
interactions.source_label: JMIR Human Factors — single older adults

interactions.title: Being jealous of a robot at the LOVOT café
interactions.description: Visitors who spread their affection between two LOVOTs find one will hold out its arms and pull a hurt face when ignored, and reviewers describe learning 'what it feels like to be jealous of a robot' as it favors whoever played with it most.

interactions.title: Snapshots of the family while you're out
interactions.description: Already taking photos of people it meets and pushing them to your phone, it could compile a quiet daily montage of who it spent time with at home — grounded in its onboard cameras, face recognition, and companion app.

interactions.title: Noticing when a housemate runs cold
interactions.description: Its thermal camera reads skin temperature to tell people from objects; that same sensor could let it gently nuzzle and check on an elderly resident who has been still and cooling for too long, flagging the caregiver through the app.
